elements.sent-2411-001: Semi-supervised learning holds a pivotal position in anomaly detection applications, yet identifying anomaly patterns with a limited number of labeled samples poses a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-002: Furthermore, the absence of interpretability poses major obstacles to the practical adoption of semi-supervised frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-003: The majority of existing interpretation techniques are tailored for supervised/unsupervised frameworks or non-security domains, falling short in providing dependable interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this research paper, we introduce SADDE, a general framework designed to accomplish two primary objectives: (1) to render the anomaly detection process interpretable and enhance the credibility of interpretation outcomes, and (2) to assign high-confidence pseudo labels to unlabeled samples, thereby boosting the performance of anomaly detection systems when supervised data is scarce.
elements.sent-2411-005: To achieve the first objective, we devise a cutting-edge interpretation method that utilizes both global and local interpreters to furnish trustworthy explanations.
elements.sent-2411-006: For the second objective, we conceptualize a novel two-stage semi-supervised learning framework tailored for network anomaly detection, ensuring that the model predictions of both stages align with specific constraints.
elements.sent-2411-007: We apply SADDE to two illustrative network anomaly detection tasks and conduct extensive evaluations in comparison with notable prior works.
elements.sent-2411-008: The experimental findings underscore that SADDE is capable of delivering precise detection results alongside dependable interpretations for semi-supervised network anomaly detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-009: The source code for SADDE is accessible at: https://github.com/M-Code-Space/SADDE.
elements.sent-2411-010: Anomaly detection has become a fundamental task in various applications, including network intrusion detection \cite{mirsky2018kitsune , web attack detection \cite{9155278 , and advanced persistent threat detection \cite{259729 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Anomaly detection systems aim to identify unforeseen threats, such as zero-day attacks.
elements.sent-2411-012: To achieve this, semi-supervised learning is increasingly promising, as it requires only limited labeled data, unlike traditional supervised approaches that depend on extensive labeled samples for training.
elements.sent-2411-013: Despite the great promise of semi-supervised network anomaly detection systems, the lack of interpretability of their predictions poses major barriers to their practical adoption.
elements.sent-2411-014: Firstly, it is challenging to build trust in the decisions made by these systems, which often provide only binary outputs (benign or malicious) without adequate justification or reliable evidence.
elements.sent-2411-015: Second, it is nearly impossible for analysts to manually interpret the decisions of these black-box machine learning models, as the volume of training data and the complexity of the learned models are beyond human understanding \cite{guidotti2018survey .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Third, minimizing false positives and miss-detected samples remains a critical challenge for anomaly detection systems in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-017: Without a clear understanding of how the models operate, it is impossible to effectively update or adjust them to reduce them.
elements.sent-2411-018: Consequently, security operators are left unable to decide whether they can trust model decisions based on excessively simple model predictions, are unwilling to use theoretical semi-supervised network anomaly detection systems in their practical applications, and feel overwhelmed by numerous meaningless false positives.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width= .8\linewidth]{figs/problem.pdf} \caption{Illustration of the divergent explanation results of different interpreters.} \label{fig:problem}
elements.sent-2411-019: Recently, some research has proposed techniques for interpreting machine learning model decisions by identifying key features that significantly influence the final prediction \cite{montavon2018methods,ribeiro2016should_lime,lundberg2017unified_shap,guo2018_lemna .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Predominant approaches include local/model-agnostic \cite{ribeiro2016should,ribeiro2018anchors,guidotti2018local,lundberg2017unified,guo2018_lemna and global \cite{fisher2019all,9817459 methods that focus on interpreting individual and clusters predictions for a given black-box classifier.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: These interpreters either try to approximate the local decision boundary using a linear algorithm to find key features of the given input, or they perturb the current input's feature values or permute features of a cluster and observe the model’s feedback to pinpoint the most influential ones.
elements.sent-2411-022: Security operators can use these interpreted key features to better understand model decisions \cite{guidotti2018survey .}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, these studies cannot be directly used for semi-supervised network anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-024: Furthermore, studies indicate that the explanation results provided by existing interpreters lack consensus \cite{fan2020can , leading to confusion regarding their trustworthiness.}
elements.sent-2411-025: To our best knowledge, although a few studies examine the reliability of existing interpreters \cite{fan2020can,yeh2019fidelity,camburu2019can , they mainly focus on developing more effective evaluation metrics rather than improving the reliability of interpreters' results.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Fig. \ref{fig:problem illustrates the process of a classifier analyzing network traffic data and producing decisions, such as identifying whether the traffic is anomalous or benign.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Various interpretability methods, including LIME, DeepLIFT, Lemna, and so on, generate different key features (e.g., "Flow Bytes/s," "Idle Max," "Fwd IAT Min"), leading to confusion for the security analyst.
elements.sent-2411-028: The inconsistency in these explanations raises uncertainty regarding the reliability of the interpretations.. Our Work.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this paper, we propose SADDE, a general semi-supervised framework for automatically learning from enormous unlabeled data and improving the reliability of interpretation results.
elements.sent-2411-030: The design goal of SADDE is to develop a novel semi-supervised learning framework for network anomaly detection applications that meets the special requirements of security domains (such as reliable, human-understandable, stable, robust, and fast).
elements.sent-2411-031: To this end, we propose two techniques in SADDE referred to as Global-local Knowledge Association Mechanism (KAM) and Two-stage Semi-supervised Learning System (ToS).
elements.sent-2411-032: KAM helps security analysts understand the underlying reason for model predictions while ToS provides an effective pseudo-labeling process to assist the semi-supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-033: Compared to original black-box machine learning models, security practitioners can better understand system feedback.
elements.sent-2411-034: Besides, with more reliable interpretations, system operators are more willing to adopt theoretical semi-supervised frameworks in their practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-035: We also provide prototype implementations of SADDE over two representative network anomaly detection datasets (ISCXTor2016 \cite{lashkari2017characterization and CIC-DoHBrw-2020 \cite{montazerishatoori2020detection ).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The proposed KAM and ToS are extensively evaluated compared to representative prior approaches.
elements.sent-2411-037: Experimental results demonstrate that SADDE can provide reliable explanations for semi-supervised learning frameworks while satisfying the requirements of security-related applications.. Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-038: This paper has the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose SADDE, a general framework for interpreting and improving semi-supervised network anomaly detection in security-related applications, which includes two key components: KAM provides reliable, human-understandable, stable, robust, and fast interpretations for semi-supervised systems and ToS makes it possible to automatically learn from large unlabeled datasets.
elements.sent-2411-040: We conduct extensive experiments to demonstrate that the proposed SADDE outperforms existing approaches regarding fidelity, stability, robustness, and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-041: The overview of SADDE is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:mainFrame consisting of two key components: Global-local Knowledge Association Mechanism (KAM) and Two-stage Semi-supervised Learning System (ToS).}
elements.sent-2411-042: ToS enables accurate semi-supervised learning via a novel two-stage pseudo-labeling process while KAM provides reliable interpretations for the semi-supervised anomaly detection system.
elements.sent-2411-043: SADDE is a general model-independent framework that formulates semi-supervised learning and decision interpretation into a unified end-to-end learning process with security-related constraints.
elements.sent-2411-044: We instantiate SADDE on the aforementioned network anomaly detection security application.
elements.sent-2411-045: With SADDE, malicious network traffic can be automatically detected without requiring large labeled datasets, meanwhile, security practitioners can better understand system behaviors and trust system decisions.. Working Process.
elements.sent-2411-046: The working process of the proposed SADDE is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-047: Before starting, we assume that there is a small labeled dataset that can be utilized to pre-train the local and global machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-048: Similar assumptions can be found in \cite{yao2018msml,apruzzese2022sok,li2024instantT .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Given an instance (or a batch of instances), we fed it into the pre-trained local model to extract its feature embedding and predict its label.
elements.sent-2411-050: The extracted feature embedding is then clustered by the global model into several clusters, where samples from the same cluster share similar characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-051: Feature representations from a cluster are treated as global representations while the feature embedding from an instance is defined as a local representation.
elements.sent-2411-052: We group the input instance into one of the clusters by comparing the distance between the embedding of the instance and the clusters.
elements.sent-2411-053: Then, the predictions of the global and local models are interpreted by the global and local interpreters, and the reliability of the interpretation results is further improved by comparing the global and local interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-054: It has a higher probability of improving its trustworthiness by matching global and local interpretations instead of two local interpreters.
elements.sent-2411-055: For instance, in scenarios where individual samples contain noise or have been maliciously altered, the interpretation results at the local level may be wrong, however, due to the fact that the global and local interpreters are heterogeneous, there is a probability that the interpretation results at the global level is correct.
elements.sent-2411-056: In this way, the interpretations' reliability could be improved.
elements.sent-2411-057: If the interpreted global and local key features match (the similarity score is higher than a given threshold), we consider the key features interpreted by the interpreters to be credible.
elements.sent-2411-058: These samples will not be utilized for model re-training to avoid possible model performance deterioration.
elements.sent-2411-059: Finally, labels predicted by the global and local models are aligned to find the most credible pseudo-labels for the next round's semi-supervised training.
elements.sent-2411-060: More details about SADDE are presented in Algorithm~\ref{alg:SADDE .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{SADDE} \label{alg:SADDE} \SetKwInOut{Input}{Input} \SetKwInOut{Output}{Output} $, local model $M_1$ and global model $M_2$, local interpreter $L$, and global interpreter $G$ } \Output{Final predictions and interpreted key features of each sample and each class. Samples with high-confidence pseudo labels. Well-trained $M_1$ and $M_2$.} \% Step 0: Model Pre-training Train $M_1$ and $M_2$ on the labeled pre-training set \While{True} { \For{Each sample (or batch of samples) in $X$} { \% Step 1: Feature Extraction Extract features of $x_i$ and predict its local pseudo label $\hat{y}_1$ by $M_1$ Group the feature embedding into a cluster by $M_2$ using $c^{(i)} = \mathop{\arg\min}\limits_{j} ||v_i - \mu_j ||^2$. Assign the label of the $j$-th cluster $c^{(i)}$ to the unlabeled data $x_i$, i.e., the global pseudo label $\hat{y}_2$. \% Step 2: Double Verification //First Verification: KAM// Interpret key features of the cluster by $G$ Interpret key features of the instance by $L$ Calculate a similarity score $sim$ between the interpreted global and local key features by $ sim(int_{x}(m),int_{y}(m'), k) = 2*\frac{int^{k}_{x}(m)\cap int^{k}_{y}(m')}{\left| int^{k}_{x}(m) \right|+\left| int^{k}_{y}(m') \right|}.$ \If{The $sim > T$} { //Second Verification: ToS// Check the predictions of $M_1$ and $M_2$ \If{$\hat{y}_1 == \hat{y}_2$} {Treat them as samples with reliable pseudo-labels and use them in the next round of model training } {Final output} } } Retrain $M_1$ and $M_2$ on the pseudo-labeled data }
elements.sent-2411-061: The global-local Knowledge Association Mechanism (KAM) takes advantage of both the local and global interpreters to provide more reliable interpretations of model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-062: It uses a local (or model-agnostic) interpreter $L$ to interpret the prediction of the local model $M_1$.
elements.sent-2411-063: The interpretation results indicate which features influence the model's prediction the most.
elements.sent-2411-064: Meanwhile, it adopts a global interpreter $G$ to explain the prediction of the global model $M_2$, and the interpreted key features represent the most important features of the cluster that the current instance belongs to.
elements.sent-2411-065: Based on the results obtained by the global interpreter $G$ and local interpreter $L$, a feature similarity calculation method (as defined in Eq.\eqref{eq:similarity ) is used to further verify the reliability of the interpretation results.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Inspired by~\cite{fan2020can , the similarity score is defined as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Different from~\cite{fan2020can , it measures the similarity between the locally interpreted key feature set of an instance and the globally interpreted key feature set of a cluster that the instance belongs to. \begin{equation}\label{eq:similarity} sim(int_{x}(m),int_{y}(m'), k) = 2*\frac{int^{k}_{x}(m)\cap int^{k}_{y}(m')}{\left| int^{k}_{x}(m) \right|+\left| int^{k}_{y}(m') \right|},\end{equation} where $int_x(m)$ is the interpretation result of the $x$-th sample generated by the interpreter $m$, and $k$ represents the interpreted top-$k$ key features of the $x$-th sample generated by the interpreter $m$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The $int_y(m')$ has a similar meaning where $m'$ is another interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-069: Similarly, $int_x^k(m)$ represents the number of top-$k$ interpretation results of the $x$-th sample interpreted by interpreter $m$.
elements.sent-2411-070: The local interpretations of an instance are considered highly reliable when the feature similarity of the local and global interpretations is higher than a given Threshold.
elements.sent-2411-071: In our case study, we utilize the DeepLift \cite{shrikumar2017learning_deeplift as our local interpreter and PFIE \cite{fisher2019all as our global interpreter as an example.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Other local/model-agnostic or global interpreters can also be used as the local and global interpreters in KAM.
elements.sent-2411-073: Besides, in security applications, a transparent decision-making process is crucial for system operators of security applications to adopt theoretical algorithms in practice.
elements.sent-2411-074: Typically, for semi-supervised learning, pseudo labels are created by a pre-trained machine learning model.
elements.sent-2411-075: In contrast with most existing semi-supervised learning methods that explore pseudo labels by only once predictions, ToS executes this process twice and the confidence of these virtual labels is improved by comparing predictions of both stages.
elements.sent-2411-076: For anomaly detection systems in security domains that only have limited labeled data, ToS repeatedly adds highly confident pseudo-labeled data for re-training the model, which significantly improves the model performance.
elements.sent-2411-077: The key idea of ToS lies in the selection of high-confidence pseudo-labeled samples and thus guarantees the subsequent process of model re-training.
elements.sent-2411-078: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:mainFrame , ToS contains a local machine learning model $M_1$ and a global model $M_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: During the pertaining phase, $M_1$ and $M_2$ are pre-trained on a smaller labeled set, making them capable of providing virtual labels to unlabeled data.
elements.sent-2411-080: During the re-training phase, firstly, $M_1$ extracts features of given instances and makes predictions.
elements.sent-2411-081: Secondly, $M_2$ clusters the feature embedding into several clusters according to feature distances \textcolor{black {(see Eq.\eqref{eq:cluster ). \begin{equation}\label{eq:cluster} c^{(i)} = \mathop{\arg\min}\limits_{j} ||v_i - \mu_j ||^2\end{equation} where $v_i$ denotes feature embedding of an unlabeled instance $x_i$, and $\mu_j$ represents centroids of class $j$ in labeled data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The goal of the double verify mechanism is to further reduce false positive and miss-detection rates and improve detection accuracy through a two-layer verification process that incorporates both the similar feature verification in KAM and the global and local prediction check in ToS.
elements.sent-2411-083: For the first-layer verification, as aforementioned in Section \ref{subsec:KAM , it compares the calculated similarity score (see Eq.\eqref{eq:similarity ), denoted as $sim$, with a predefined threshold $T$.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: We consider the interpretations to be reliable if $sim>T$.
elements.sent-2411-085: To some extent, it indicates that the sample has not been modified or attacked and could be used for the next round of re-training.
elements.sent-2411-086: For the second-layer verification, we consider the generated pseudo labels to be confident when the local model's prediction $\hat{y _l$ matches the global model's prediction $\hat{y _g$.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The selected reliable samples and their pseudo-labels are used to retrain both the global and clustering models.
elements.sent-2411-088: The model then waits for new network flow data to arrive for further processing.
elements.sent-2411-089: In our case study, we use the Multilayer Perceptron Network (MLP) \cite{rumelhart1986learning and DBSCAN \cite{ester1996density as our local and global models respectively as an example for evaluating the effectiveness of the proposed ToS.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Other machine learning models can also be chosen accordingly for specific tasks.

elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, in subsection~\ref{subsec:result , we compare the proposed KAM and ToS with five and eight state-of-the-art on two representative network anomaly detection datasets and analyze their results.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Two widely used network anomaly detection datasets, ISCXTor2016 \cite{lashkari2017characterization and CIC-DoHBrw-2020 \cite{montazerishatoori2020detection , are employed to evaluate the performance of SADDE.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: ISCXTor2016 contains Tor and non-Tor network flows in various applications and each instance has 28 features.
elements.sent-2411-097: It consists of 68,191 samples, of which 56,534 are non-Tor and 11,657 are Tor samples.
elements.sent-2411-098: CIC-DoHBrw-2020 contains labeled traffic generated by DNS over HTTPS (DoH) protocols.
elements.sent-2411-099: It is a dataset for detecting browser-based DoH activities and studying secure communication anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-101: There are 933,189 instances, including 216,649 DoH and 716,540 non-DoH samples.
elements.sent-2411-102: For the ISCXTor2016 dataset, the first 1\% of the data is used as the pre-training set (i.e., small labeled set), the last 20\% as the testing set, and the next 20\% as the validation set, while the remaining data is allocated for re-training.
elements.sent-2411-103: The CSIC-DoHBrw-2020 dataset follows a similar partitioning structure, except that the first 0.5\% of the data is used as the pre-training set.
elements.sent-2411-104: The experiments are conducted using Python (v3.8), along with the PyTorch framework (v1.11.0), CUDA (v11.3), and Scikit-learn (v1.3.2) to develop and evaluate SADDE.
elements.sent-2411-105: A three-layer FCN is employed as our $M_1$, and each layer contains 256 neurons.
elements.sent-2411-106: During training, the Adam optimizer is used with a learning rate of $1e-3$.
elements.sent-2411-107: A dropout rate of 0.3 is implemented to avoid over-fitting, and cross-entropy loss was selected as the objective function for classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-108: We set the batch size to 64.
elements.sent-2411-109: DBSCAN was employed with an epsilon value of 0.5 and a minimum sample size of 1 for clustering, ensuring that all samples could potentially be included in a cluster.
elements.sent-2411-110: The output of FCN's third fully connected layer is utilized as the input of DBSCAN.
elements.sent-2411-111: Additionally, early stopping was implemented with a patience of 5 epochs during both the pre-training and re-training phases so that the training process could be halted if there is no improvement in the validation loss over 5 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-112: In PFIE, each feature is permuted by 100 times.
elements.sent-2411-113: For DeepLift, the eps parameter is configured to $1e-10$.
elements.sent-2411-114: The number of key features is specified as 10, and the threshold for the similarity score is established at 0.6.
elements.sent-2411-115: All simulations are executed on a Linux server powered by an AMD EPYC 7282 16-Core Processor (2.80 GHz) and 377 GB of RAM.
elements.sent-2411-116: To ensure consistency, all algorithms are run within the same environment, and for fairness, the average results from 10 runs are recorded for comparison.. Benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-117: We employ a comprehensive set of benchmarks in this work to validate the performance of SADDE.
elements.sent-2411-118: Firstly, we conduct a comparative analysis of the proposed KAM against eight state-of-the-art interpreters, including Lime \cite{ribeiro2016should_lime , SHAP \cite{lundberg2017unified_shap , DeepLift \cite{shrikumar2017learning_deeplift , COIN \cite{liu2017contextual , DiCE \cite{mothilal2020explaining , xNIDS \cite{wei2023xnids , CADE \cite{yang2021cade , Anchors \cite{ribeiro2018anchors .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Then, we compared the introduced ToS with five state-of-the-art semi-supervised methods, including InstantT \cite{li2024instantT , ACR \cite{wei2023towards_ACR , Clustering* \cite{Fini_2023_CVPR_clustering , LVM \cite{min2024leveraging_LVM , and M3S \cite{sun2020multi_M3S .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: We use Accuracy (Acc.), Precision (Pre.), Recall (Rec.), F1-score (F1.), False Alarm Rate (FAR), Missed Detection Rate (MDR), and standardized partial AUC (SPAUC) \cite{mcclish1989analyzing to measure different semi-supervised models' performance.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Particularly, SPAUC measures the performance of a model within a specific region of the ROC curve, focusing on a segment that is most relevant under conditions of class imbalance and differential costs of misclassification.
elements.sent-2411-122: FAR reflects the amount of normal network traffic that is wrongly classified as abnormal while MDR indicates how much anomaly network traffic that are miss-detected by the models.
elements.sent-2411-123: The higher the SPAUC, the better the performance is.
elements.sent-2411-124: The lower the FAR and MDR, the better the performance is.
elements.sent-2411-125: Here we use $SPAUC_{FPR \leq 0.05}$ for all experiments.
elements.sent-2411-126: Additionally, we evaluate the performance of the proposed KAM interpreter in terms of fidelity, stability, robustness, efficiency, and AOPC, and compare it with the state-of-the-art interpreters.

elements.sent-2411-127: To evaluate the fidelity of an interpreter, we define an indicator similar to that used in \cite{han2021deepaid , called the Label Flipping Rate (LFR), which is the ratio of samples that change to a different class after being modified based on the interpretation results.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Specifically, in the context of a classification task, the interpreter identifies several key features.
elements.sent-2411-129: The values of these key features are replaced with the average values corresponding to the opposite class, and a new prediction is generated to determine whether the label has been changed.
elements.sent-2411-130: The LFR is mathematically expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:fidelity} LFR=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} I(f'(x_i) \neq f(x_i))}{n}\end{equation} where $n$ denotes the total number of samples, $f(x_i)$ represents the original predicted label for a sample $x_i$, $f'(x_i)$ is the predicted label after modification, and $I(\cdot)$ is an indicator function that equals 1 if the label has flipped and 0 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-131: This metric captures the percentage of samples for which the predicted label changes after altering the key features.
elements.sent-2411-132: The stability of interpreters refers to the consistency of interpretation results for the same samples across multiple runs.
elements.sent-2411-133: Given two interpretation outcomes $int_x(m)_1$ and $int_x(m)_2$, their similarity can be calculated using Eq.\eqref{eq:similarity .}
elements.sent-2411-134: This process is repeated to assess the similarity between the interpretation results from two runs under identical settings for each sample, and the overall stability is calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:stability} Stability = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sim}(int_x(m)_1^i, int_x(m)_2^i)\end{equation} In this equation, $n$ is the total number of samples, and $\text{sim (int_x(m)_1^i, int_x(m)_2^i)$ represents the similarity between the two interpretation outcomes for the same sample $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: The average similarity across all samples is calculated to quantify the stability of the interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-136: Robustness refers to the similarity of interpretation results for the same sample before and after adding noise, sampled from a Gaussian distribution $N(0, \sigma^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-137: Specifically, for each sample $i$, the robustness is calculated as the average similarity between the interpretation result without noise, $int_x(m)_i$, and the interpretation result after adding noise, $int_x(m + \epsilon)_i$, where $\epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-138: The overall robustness is computed using the equation: \begin{equation}Robustness = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sim}(int_x(m)_i, int_x(m + \epsilon)_i, k)\end{equation} Here, $k$ represents the number of key features.
elements.sent-2411-139: In this work, the noise is set to $\sigma = 0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-140: We assess efficiency by recording the runtime required to interpret 2000 samples for each interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-142: Table \ref{tb:PretrainPerformanceTor and Table \ref{tb:PretrainPerformanceDoH present the performance of ToS when trained on the pre-training set (denoted as \ding{172 ) and when trained on both the pre-training and re-training sets (denoted as \ding{173 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: The results indicate that the re-training approach (\ding{173 ) achieves significantly better performance than pre-training alone (\ding{172 ), with improvements of approximately 20\%, 10\%, and 14\% in Precision, FAR, and SPAUC, on the ISCXTor2016 dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Similarly, on the CIC-DoHBrw-2020 dataset, the re-training process (\ding{173 ) enhances the model's performance by about 14\%, 26\%, and 4\% in terms of F1-score, MDR, and SPAUC, compared to training only on the smaller pre-training set (\ding{172 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-145: These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the semi-supervised learning process in improving the performance of the proposed SADDE.

elements.sent-2411-179: In this section, we briefly discuss existing literature from two aspects.. Interpreters.
elements.sent-2411-180: Numerous studies have been conducted to interpret machine learning models and achieved promising results, which can be roughly categorized into two groups, i.e., local/model-agnostic \cite{ribeiro2016should,ribeiro2018anchors,guidotti2018local,lundberg2017unified,guo2018_lemna,fong2019understanding and global interpreters \cite{fisher2019all,9817459 .}}
elements.sent-2411-181: The former interprets individual predictions for a given machine learning model while the latter attempts to explain model predictions of a cluster.
elements.sent-2411-182: Local/model-agnostic interpreters are mostly perturbation-based, Gradient-based, and others.
elements.sent-2411-183: Perturbation-based approaches \cite{fong2019understanding,fong2017interpretable,yang2021cade perturb data features and check the variation of model predictions to identify the most important ones.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Gradient-based approaches \cite{shrikumar2017learning_deeplift,selvaraju2017grad back-propagate gradients through the model to evaluate each feature's sensitivity.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Others treat the target machine learning model as a black-box \cite{ribeiro2016should_lime,ribeiro2018anchors .}
elements.sent-2411-186: Techniques like LIME \cite{ribeiro2016should_lime , LIMNA \cite{guo2018_lemna , and SHAP \cite{lundberg2017unified_shap attempt to utilize a linear model to approximate the decision boundary of the input, and utilize it to find the most influential features.}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Global interpreters like \cite{fisher2019all explain model prediction by randomly permuting specific feature values of a cluster and observer model predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-188: The authors of \cite{9817459 developed an interpreter for explaining clustering algorithms by formulating clustering decisions as being functionally equivalent neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Few studies aim to interpret unsupervised models such as COIN \cite{liu2017contextual and CADE \cite{yang2021cade which are used as baselines in our experiments.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: In addition to them, most of other works directly use the interpreters proposed for supervised learning to unsupervised learning applications.
elements.sent-2411-191: The work of \cite{fan2020can demonstrated that the explanation results provided by existing interpreters lack consensus, resulting in confusion regarding their trustworthiness.}
elements.sent-2411-192: There are a few studies that examine the reliability of existing interpreters \cite{yeh2019fidelity,camburu2019can,fan2020can , for example, \cite{yeh2019fidelity introduced two metrics to evaluate interpreters, i.e., sensitivity and infidelity.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: An off-the-shelf evaluation test is proposed for post-hoc interpreters in view of feature selection in \cite{camburu2019can .}
elements.sent-2411-194: The work of \cite{9230374 developed criteria for evaluating and comparing different interpreters, including both general properties like accuracy and security-related ones, such as robustness, efficiency, and completeness.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Unfortunately, to our best knowledge, there is no existing work exploring how to improve the reliability of interpretation results.. Semi-supervised Learning.
elements.sent-2411-196: A recent surge of interest has focused on anomaly detection with semi-supervised learning, which attempts to assign high-confidence pseudo-labels to unlabeled data.
elements.sent-2411-197: There is a wide range of research on semi-supervised learning, which generally falls into two categories, i.e., single-stage-based and multi-stage-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-198: Some single-stage-based semi-supervised learning techniques improve the confidence of pseudo labels based on thresholds \cite{li2024instantT,min2024leveraging_LVM , and they usually set high thresholds to unlabeled samples to prevent the occurrence of incorrect pseudo labels, or contrastive learning \cite{Fini_2023_CVPR_clustering,wei2023towards_ACR that combines combine pseudo labeling and consistency regularization to encourages similar predictions between two different views of an instance to improve the robustness of the model.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Unlike single-stage-based semi-supervised learning techniques, multi-stage-based semi-supervised learning approaches, such as \cite{sun2020multi_M3S,sun2020multi , explore the most confident pseudo labels by using multiple models and improve their confidence by aligning the predictions from each stage.}
elements.sent-2411-200: In this paper, we propose SADDE, a general framework aiming to interpret and improve semi-supervised anomaly detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-201: It incorporates two key techniques KAM and ToS: (1) KAM provides reliable, human-understandable, stable, robust, and fast interpretations for semi-supervised systems; (2) ToS makes it possible to automatically learn from large unlabeled datasets.
elements.sent-2411-202: By applying and evaluating Adaptive NAD over two classical network anomaly detection datasets, we demonstrate that SADDE can provide fast and accurate anomaly detection results as well as reliable, human-understandable, stable, robust, and fast interpretations.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}
elements.sent-2411-203: During the localization process in wireless networks, risks such as Sybil attacks and nodes' location privacy leakage can exist due to the open and shared nature of wireless networks.
elements.sent-2411-204: However, it is challenging to obtain accurate location for sensor nodes while preserving the privacy of observer nodes who assist in sensor node localization while in the presence of Sybil attacks.
elements.sent-2411-205: Therefore, in this paper, we propose a secure and private localization algorithm, PrSLoc, based on Differential Privacy and Approximate Point-In-Triangulation (APIT, a range-free positioning algorithm).
elements.sent-2411-206: Specifically, APIT is applied for sensor nodes' localization based on the received signal strength (RSS) from observer nodes as APIT doesn't require special hardware or additional devices to estimate node positions.
elements.sent-2411-207: Besides, Differential Privacy technique is utilized to protect the privacy of observer nodes' location information (i.e., nodes' identity, location, and transmission power).
elements.sent-2411-208: Additionally, we mitigate Sybil attacks using a novel lightweight Sybil detection approach introduced in this paper that is based on the twice difference of the perceived RSS.
elements.sent-2411-209: Simulation results demonstrate that PrSLoc can efficiently eliminate Sybil nodes while preserving the observers' privacy during wireless localization processes.
elements.sent-2411-210: Furthermore, the computation overhead and storage cost of the proposed PrSLoc are competitive compared with existing work.
elements.sent-2411-211: Our code can be found here: https://github.com/learning-lemon/PrSLoc-Sybil-Attack-Detection-for-Localization-with-Private-Observers-using-Differential-Privacy.git.
elements.sent-2411-212: Semi-supervised Learning plays a crucial role in network anomaly detection applications, however, learning anomaly patterns with limited labeled samples is not easy.
elements.sent-2411-213: Additionally, the lack of interpretability creates key barriers to the adoption of semi-supervised frameworks in practice.
elements.sent-2411-214: Most existing interpretation methods are developed for supervised/unsupervised frameworks or non-security domains and fail to provide reliable interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-215: In this paper, we propose AnomalyAID, a general framework aiming to (1) make the anomaly detection process interpretable and improve the reliability of interpretation results, and (2) assign high-confidence pseudo labels to unlabeled samples for improving the performance of anomaly detection systems with limited supervised data.
elements.sent-2411-216: For (1), we propose a novel interpretation approach that leverages global and local interpreters to provide reliable explanations, while for (2), we design a new two-stage semi-supervised learning framework for network anomaly detection by aligning both stages' model predictions with special constraints.
elements.sent-2411-217: We apply AnomalyAID over two representative network anomaly detection tasks and extensively evaluate AnomalyAID with representative prior works.
elements.sent-2411-218: Experimental results demonstrate that AnomalyAID can provide accurate detection results with reliable interpretations for semi-supervised network anomaly detection systems.

elements.sent-2411-228: We propose AnomalyAID, a general framework for interpreting and improving semi-supervised network anomaly detection in security-related applications, which includes two key components: KAM provides reliable interpretations for semi-supervised systems and ToS makes it possible to learn from large unlabeled datasets automatically.

elements.sent-2411-231: The machine learning model is trained in a supervised or semi-/un-supervised manner.
elements.sent-2411-232: After training the detector, the security analyst analyzes incoming samples and classifies them as either malicious or benign.
elements.sent-2411-233: However, since the model operates as a black box, it doesn’t provide any reasoning behind its decisions.
elements.sent-2411-234: To understand why the system flagged a sample, the analyst turns to explainability methods that highlight critical features of the input.

elements.sent-2411-245: In the process of detecting network anomalies (i.e., classifying network traffic into malicious or benign traffic), each network traffic flow $x_i$ is represented as a feature vector in a $d$-dimensional space, where $x_i \in \mathbb{R ^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-246: A network anomaly detector $f$ can be developed using a designated supervised or semi-/un-supervised machine learning algorithm based on this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-247: We can express the prediction for the $i$-th sample as $f(x_i) = \hat{y _i$, where $\hat{y _i$ reflects the predicted output associated with $x_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-248: The classifier is considered to perform correctly if the predicted label $\hat{y _i$ aligns with the actual label $y_i$.. Interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-249: The interpretation process is denoted as $g = m(f)$, where $f$ is a specific pre-trained network anomaly detector and $m$ is an interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-250: Given a network traffic flow $x_i$, the interpreter $m$ will produce the interpretation result $g$, which is represented as a list of features that are ranked based on their contribution to the final decision.
elements.sent-2411-251: PFIE, proposed by \cite{fisher2019all , is a simple and easy-to-implement feature importance calculation method used to explain the predictions of machine learning models.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Its core idea is to evaluate the importance of a feature to the model's predictions by observing the change in the model's prediction error after shuffling (permuting) the values of that feature.
elements.sent-2411-253: Specifically, the calculation of PI first involves computing the baseline error using the trained model and the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-254: Then, a specific feature is selected, and its values are randomly shuffled to break the relationship between the feature and the target variable.
elements.sent-2411-255: Next, the model's prediction error is recalculated using the shuffled dataset.
elements.sent-2411-256: Finally, the importance of the feature is assessed by comparing the baseline error with the shuffled error (as defined in Eq., \eqref{eq:PFIE ).}
elements.sent-2411-257: The greater the increase in error, the more important the feature is to the model's predictions. \begin{equation}\label{eq:PFIE} Importance(f) = Error(M) - Error(M_p),\end{equation} where $Importance(f)$ means the permutation importance of feature $f$, indicating the impact of the feature $f$ on the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-258: The larger the value, the more important the feature.
elements.sent-2411-259: It is introduced by \cite{shrikumar2017learning_deeplift , which is designed to interpret the output predictions of neural networks by comparing the activation of each neuron to its “reference activation” and decomposing the contributions of all neurons in the network to the input features through backpropagation.}
elements.sent-2411-260: In the context of network traffic anomaly detection, the process begins with selecting a zero-input as the reference input to compute the difference in activations.
elements.sent-2411-261: Typically, the reference input is chosen to be a zero vector (i.e., all feature values are set to zero), representing a state of “no features.” For instance, \( x_0 = 0 \).
elements.sent-2411-262: Next, both the reference input \( x_0 \) and the actual input \( x_i \) are propagated through the deep learning-based network traffic anomaly detection model to compute the reference activation \( y_0 \) and the actual activation \( y_i \) for each neuron.
elements.sent-2411-263: The difference between the reference activations and the actual activations is then calculated for each neuron.
elements.sent-2411-264: Subsequently, DeepLift rules—such as the Linear Rule, Rescale Rule, or RevealCancel Rule—are applied to assign contribution scores for each input feature (e.g., pixel) to the output.
elements.sent-2411-265: Finally, the contribution scores are backpropagated from the output layer to the input layer using the chain rule.
elements.sent-2411-266: Through this approach, DeepLift provides a clear interpretation of how the CNN model utilizes input features for decision-making, thereby enhancing the model's interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-267: DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) \cite{ester1996density is a popular clustering algorithm that focuses on identifying clusters based on the density of data points.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Unlike traditional clustering methods like K-means, DBSCAN does not require the number of clusters to be specified in advance and can discover clusters of arbitrary shapes while effectively handling noise.
elements.sent-2411-269: Specifically, it calculates the density around each sample using two parameters: the neighborhood radius $\epsilon$ and the minimum number of points $MinPts$ required to form a dense region.
elements.sent-2411-270: The Euclidean distance between two samples $G(x_i)$ and $G(x_k)$ is denoted as $\|G(x_i) - G(x_k)\|$.
elements.sent-2411-271: The $\epsilon$-neighborhood of a sample $x_i$ is defined as: \begin{equation}N_\epsilon(x_i) = \{x_j \mid \|G(x_i) - G(x_j)\| \leq \epsilon \},\end{equation} where $N_\epsilon(x_i)$ represents the set of points within the $\epsilon$-neighborhood of $x_i$.
elements.sent-2411-272: If the number of points in the neighborhood, $|N_\epsilon(x_i)|$, is at least $MinPts$, the sample $x_i$ is classified as a core point.
elements.sent-2411-273: Samples reachable from a core point but not meeting the density requirement are labeled as border points, while samples that are not reachable from any core point are treated as outliers.. FCN.
elements.sent-2411-274: A Fully Connected Network (FCN) \cite{rumelhart1986learning is a type of neural network architecture where each neuron in one layer is connected to every neuron in the next layer.}
elements.sent-2411-275: This architecture is commonly used for tasks such as network traffic anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-276: The key ideas and principles of an FCN revolve around the transformation of input data through multiple layers of neurons, with each layer learning increasingly abstract representations of the input.
elements.sent-2411-277: An FCN consists of multiple layers, including input layers, hidden layers, and an output layer.
elements.sent-2411-278: Each layer is fully connected to the next layer, meaning every neuron in one layer is connected to every neuron in the subsequent layer (the calculation is shown in Eq. \eqref{eq:FC ). \begin{equation}\label{eq:FC} \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{x}^{(l)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{W ^{(l)}$ and $\mathbf{b ^{(l)}$ are the weight matrix and bias vector of layer $l$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-279: Each neuron in an FCN computes a weighted sum of its inputs and applies a non-linear activation function (e.g., ReLU) to introduce non-linearity into the model.
elements.sent-2411-280: This allows the network to learn complex patterns in the data.
elements.sent-2411-281: FCNs are trained using backpropagation, where the error between the predicted output and the true label is propagated backward through the network to update the weights.
elements.sent-2411-282: This process minimizes a loss function, usually cross-entropy for classification (see Eq. \eqref{eq:CE ). \begin{equation}\label{eq:CE} \mathcal{L} = -\sum_i t_i \log(y_i),\end{equation} where $t_i$ is the true label and $y_i$ is the predicted probability for class $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-283: In our study, we use the PFIE and DeepLift as our global and local interpreters, and FCN and DBSCAN as our local and global models respectively as an example for evaluating the effectiveness of the proposed ToS.

Well-trained $M_1$ and $M_2$.} \% Step 0: Model Pre-training Train $M_1$ and $M_2$ on the labeled pre-training set \While{True} { \For{Each sample (or batch of samples) in $X$} { \% Step 1: Feature Extraction Extract features of $x_i$ and predict its local pseudo label $\hat{y}_1$ by $M_1$ Group the feature embedding into a cluster by $M_2$ using $c^{(i)} = \mathop{\arg\min}\limits_{j} ||v_i - \mu_j ||^2$. Assign the label of the $j$-th cluster $c^{(i)}$ to the unlabeled data $x_i$, i.e., the global pseudo label $\hat{y}_2$. \% Step 2: Double Verification //First Verification: KAM// Interpret key features of the cluster by $G$ Interpret key features of the instance by $L$ Calculate a similarity score $sim$ between the interpreted global and local key features by $ sim(int_{x}(m),int_{y}(m'), k) = 2*\frac{int^{k}_{x}(m)\cap int^{k}_{y}(m')}{\left| int^{k}_{x}(m) \right|+\left| int^{k}_{y}(m') \right|}.$ \If{The $sim > T$} { //Second Verification: ToS// Check the predictions of $M_1$ and $M_2$ \If{$\hat{y}_1 == \hat{y}_2$} {Treat them as samples with reliable pseudo-labels and use them in the next round of model training } {Final output} } } Retrain $M_1$ and $M_2$ on the pseudo-labeled data }
elements.sent-2411-294: Following~\cite{fan2020can , the similarity score is defined as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-295: It measures the similarity between the locally interpreted key feature set of an instance and the globally interpreted key feature set of a cluster that the instance belongs to. \begin{equation}\label{eq:similarity} sim(int_{x}(m),int_{y}(m'), k) = 2*\frac{int^{k}_{x}(m)\cap int^{k}_{y}(m')}{\left| int^{k}_{x}(m) \right|+\left| int^{k}_{y}(m') \right|},\end{equation} where $int_x(m)$ is the interpretation result of the $x$-th sample generated by the interpreter $m$, and $k$ represents the interpreted top-$k$ key features of the $x$-th sample generated by the interpreter $m$.
elements.sent-2411-296: The goal of the double verify mechanism is to further reduce false positive and miss-detection rates and improve detection accuracy through a two-layer verification process that incorporates similar feature verification in KAM and global and local prediction checks in ToS.

elements.sent-2411-364: In this paper, we propose AnomalyAID, a general framework aiming to interpret and improve semi-supervised anomaly detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-365: It incorporates two key techniques KAM and ToS: (1) KAM provides reliable interpretations for semi-supervised systems; (2) ToS makes it possible to automatically learn from large unlabeled datasets.
elements.sent-2411-366: By applying and evaluating Adaptive NAD over two classical network anomaly detection datasets, we demonstrate that AnomalyAID can provide fast and accurate anomaly detection results as well as reliable interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-367: As part of future work, researchers can further investigate the performance of AnomalyAID on various anomaly detection scenarios with different model combinations under extreme data distributions.

elements.sent-2411-370: Recently, some research has proposed techniques for interpreting machine learning model decisions by identifying key features that significantly influence the final prediction \cite{ribeiro2016should_lime,lundberg2017unified_shap,guo2018_lemna .}
elements.sent-2411-371: Predominant approaches include local/model-agnostic \cite{ribeiro2016should,ribeiro2018anchors,lundberg2017unified_shap,guo2018_lemna and global \cite{fisher2019all,9817459 methods that focus on interpreting individual and cluster predictions for a given black-box classifier.}}
elements.sent-2411-372: Existing work like \cite{fong2017interpretable,shrikumar2017learning,9817459,mothilal2020explaining,shrikumar2017learning_deeplift,han2021deepaid,wei2023xnids predominantly focuses on interpreting supervised/unsupervised models in various applications.}
elements.sent-2411-373: However, the field of developing reliable interpretation for semi-supervised network anomaly detection remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-374: Various interpretability methods, including LIME \cite{ribeiro2016should_lime , DeepLIFT \cite{shrikumar2017learning_deeplift , and Lemna \cite{guo2018_lemna , generate different key features (e.g., "Flow Bytes/s," "Idle Max," and "Fwd IAT Min"), leading to confusion for the security analyst.}}}
elements.sent-2411-375: The design goal of AnomalyAID is to develop a novel semi-supervised learning framework for network anomaly detection applications that meets the special requirements of security domains (such as reliability).
elements.sent-2411-376: KAM helps security analysts understand the underlying reason for model predictions, while ToS provides an effective pseudo-labeling process to assist the semi-supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-377: Experimental results demonstrate that AnomalyAID can provide reliable explanations for semi-supervised learning frameworks while satisfying the requirements of security-related applications (Our code is available at: https://github.com/M-Code-Space/AnomalyAID.).. elements.sent-2411-383: Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN) \cite{ester1996density is a popular clustering algorithm that focuses on identifying clusters based on the density of data points.}
elements.sent-2411-384: Unlike traditional clustering methods like K-Means, DBSCAN does not require the number of clusters to be specified in advance and can discover clusters of arbitrary shapes while effectively handling noise.
elements.sent-2411-385: In our study, we use the PFIE and DeepLift as our global and local interpreters, and FCN and DBSCAN as our local and global models, respectively, as an example for evaluating the effectiveness of the proposed ToS.

elements.sent-2411-407: Numerous studies have been conducted to interpret machine learning models and achieved promising results, which can be roughly categorized into two groups, i.e., local/model-agnostic \cite{ribeiro2016should,ribeiro2018anchors,lundberg2017unified_shap,guo2018_lemna,fong2019understanding and global interpreters \cite{fisher2019all,9817459 .}}
elements.sent-2411-408: The former interprets individual predictions for a given machine learning model, while the latter attempts to explain model predictions of a cluster.
elements.sent-2411-409: Gradient-based approaches \cite{shrikumar2017learning_deeplift back-propagate gradients through the model to evaluate each feature's sensitivity.}
elements.sent-2411-410: Global interpreters like \cite{fisher2019all explain model predictions by randomly permuting specific feature values of a cluster and observer model predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-411: Few studies aim to interpret unsupervised models such as COIN \cite{liu2017contextual_coin and CADE \cite{yang2021cade , which are used as baselines in our experiments.}}
elements.sent-2411-412: In addition to them, most of the other works directly use the interpreters proposed for supervised learning to unsupervised learning applications.
elements.sent-2411-413: There are a few studies that examine the reliability of existing interpreters \cite{yeh2019fidelity,fan2020can , for example, the authors of \cite{yeh2019fidelity introduced two metrics to evaluate interpreters, i.e., sensitivity and infidelity.}}
elements.sent-2411-414: Fan et al. \cite{fan2020can introduced three evaluation metrics to measure interpreters' stability, effectiveness, and robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-415: It incorporates two key techniques, KAM and ToS: (1) KAM provides reliable interpretations for semi-supervised systems; (2) ToS makes it possible to automatically learn from large unlabeled datasets.
